summary: McKenzie alerts readers to a commonly overlooked IRS tax credit called the Telephone Excise Tax Refund, available to Americans who paid for long distance service between 2003 and 2006. He explains the two calculation methods and recommends taking the standard refund since most people don't keep old phone bills. The post walks through the exact lines on the 1040 and 1040EZ where the credit should be entered. He emphasizes that this free money won't appear automatically on your return.
key_points: The IRS offered a Telephone Excise Tax Refund credit for long distance service paid between Feb 2003 and Aug 2006
Standard refund amounts range from $30 to $60 based on number of exemptions claimed
The credit must be manually claimed on your tax return — it won't be added automatically
Taking the standard refund is simpler than gathering old phone bills for exact calculation
money_quotes: Its very easy to forget, particularly if you're doing your own taxes, but the IRS is offering a credit against your taxes (or a refund if you don't owe taxes) if you paid for long distance service in the US.
Don't have your phone bills? Yeah, me neither. So take the standard refund.
It won't be automatically added to your return if you or your tax adviser don't put it there!
key_insight: Small tax credits like the Telephone Excise Tax Refund are easy to overlook but free money if you remember to claim them on your return.
